result:
I keep thinking about the word “threshold.” Not the magazine, not the plank under your door, but the living idea of it: a membrane, a hesitation, a place where the air pressure changes and the ears pop. Every life is a series of thresholds, yet we pretend they are rare—wedding arches, graduation walks, hospital doors. In truth we are crossing them all day long. You step into a shower and the temperature decides for you whether this will be a benevolent day or a day when the gods are petty. You open the refrigerator and the little light that has burned since 1997 either greets you or burns out in that instant, a tiny star collapsing. We hardly ever mark these things, yet something in us keeps tally. I picture a quiet bureaucrat of the soul, seated at a roll-top desk, rubber-stamping each crossing: “Threshold #46,872: left sock first today, omen uncertain.”

When I was seven I decided the doorway between the kitchen and the living room in my grandmother’s house was a portal. If I stepped through it without touching the metal strip that separated linoleum from carpet, I could wish for something and it would come true before supper. I spent an entire July afternoon hopping back and forth, barefoot, whispering “pony, ten dollars, snow in summer, parents who never shout.” The pony never arrived, but my mother did lean in and say, “Stop that thumping, you’re scaring the cat.” I concluded the cat must be the gatekeeper, a feline Cerberus who resented my negotiation tactics. Years later I learned that the strip was actually brass, not iron, and therefore useless against fairies. By then I had larger disappointments to catalog, but the memory lingers like a bruise I press when I need to feel childish again.

There is a neurological condition—prosopagnosia, face blindness—in which the ability to recognize human faces is disrupted. I sometimes think I have a mild form of threshold blindness: I cross them without noticing, then spend decades wondering why the emotional weather has changed. The day I stopped being a child was not the day I bled for the first time but the afternoon my father asked me to hold the flashlight steady while he re-wired the ceiling fan. I was fourteen, and the plastic casing of that flashlight had a hairline crack running right above the switch. My thumb kept sliding into it, and the metal beneath was hot. The fan blades spun like a helicopter waiting for clearance, and my father’s voice floated up: “Keep it steady, this is important.” In that moment I understood that he needed me, that the universe had tilted and made me essential. I did not tell anyone, not even my best friend, that I had become a woman not through blood but through electricity, through the responsibility of keeping someone else out of the dark.

After my divorce I moved into a second-floor apartment whose staircase had thirteen steps. I counted them every time, a ritual against falling. One night, drunk on self-pity and cheap merlot, I miscounted and slammed my shin into the thirteenth step. The pain was sudden and clean, like a new sheet of paper. I sat there on the stairs, cradling my leg, and laughed until I cried because the number was exactly what I needed: an unlucky thing that turned out to be true. The next morning the bruise had the exact purple of the dress I wore on my first date with my ex. I took it as a sign to burn the dress, but I am too superstitious to destroy fabric that once held happiness. Instead I packed it away with the other relics: concert tickets, a pressed violet, the marriage certificate printed on cream-colored card stock that felt like an invitation to a party that never happened. I labeled the box “Museum of Almost.”

People think resilience is a heroic quality, but mostly it is bureaucratic. You fill out forms in your sleep: “Reason for waking: nightmare/siren/bladder.” You file appeals to the universe: “Please restore my sense of wonder, address not found.” You pay fines for late apologies and accumulate penalties for unfulfilled promises to your younger self. The younger self is a persistent plaintiff; she keeps showing up in court wearing that purple bruise and waving the flashlight with the hairline crack. “Your honor,” she says, “he promised to keep me safe from the dark.” And the older self, the one with the job and the 401(k) and the sensible shoes, rolls her eyes and mutters, “I was busy, I was rewiring the ceiling fan of my life, I was holding the light for someone else.”

Lately I have been thinking about the elasticity of time. Not in the Einsteinian sense—I leave that to people who can do math without counting on their fingers—but in the way a Tuesday in February can feel like a year while a summer vacation goes by like a sneeze. I read that the brain records new experiences in detail but familiar ones in shorthand. That is why the drive to a new place feels longer than the drive back. The brain is taking notes, sketching the landscape, while on the return trip it shrugs and says, “Yeah, I got this.” Maybe that is why childhood feels so long: every day is a new country. Adulthood is a daily commute to a country whose flag you designed yourself, and you are too tired to notice you have crossed the border a thousand times.

I signed up for a pottery class because I read that clay is good for grief. The instructor, a woman named Dolores who wore her silver hair in a braid thick as a coaxial cable, said, “Center the clay, then let it center you.” I spent eight weeks making wobbly ashtrays no one smokes anymore. On the last day Dolores took my latest creation—a bowl with the approximate symmetry of a drunken heart—and turned it over in her hands. “This has good energy,” she said. I wanted to ask if good energy could compensate for bad technique, but I held my tongue. Instead I took the bowl home and placed it on the kitchen table. It sits there still, empty, a bowl that refuses to be useful. I like it that way. It is a monument to the art of almost getting it right.

My mother calls every Sunday at 9:03 a.m. She has done this for twenty-three years, even the year I lived in Tokyo. She figures out the time difference and calls anyway, a small act of maternal defiance against the international date line. We talk about the weather and the price of gasoline and whether my brother will ever settle down with someone nice. We never talk about the ceiling fan or the flashlight or the bruise that bloomed on my shin like a midnight violet. We never talk about the marriage certificate or the Museum of Almost. Instead she tells me about the neighbor’s cat, which has learned to open screen doors by hooking its paw around the handle and pulling. “Smart as a whip,” she says. I picture the cat sailing through thresholds like a furry astronaut, unbothered by the physics of belonging. I want to be that cat, but I am more like the screen door, always needing something to push against.

I read a study that says people who talk to themselves are more intelligent. I rehearse this fact while grocery shopping, whispering “avocado, oat milk, existential dread” as I push the cart. A child stares at me like I am a wizard who can summon bananas with a spell. I wink at him and he hides behind his mother’s leg. I wonder what threshold he is practicing for—maybe the first day of school, maybe the moment he realizes his parents are just people who got tired one day and never quite recovered. I want to tell him it gets better, but that is a lie. It gets different, which is not the same thing. Better is a word we use to describe the moment the flashlight gets steadier, not when the darkness disappears.

Dolores from pottery sent an email blast announcing a new session titled “Grief and Gratitude: Building Bowls for Both.” I delete it, then retrieve it from the trash, then delete it again. I do not want to build a bowl for grief; I want to pour it down the drain and watch the pipes groan under the weight. I want to pour it into the ocean and watch the tide carry it back with a seaweed garnish. I want to pour it into your coffee and watch you sip it and say, “Hmm, tastes like regret with a hint of cinnamon.” But grief is not a liquid; it is a solid that expands in the heat and cracks the container. It is the reason I count steps and avoid thirteen and sit on stairs counting bruises like rosary beads made of midnight.

Last week I dreamed of the ceiling fan again. This time it was spinning slowly, like a lazy carousel, and each blade had a word written on it: forgive, forget, forego, forgo, forgo. I noticed the repetition and tried to scrape off the extra “forgive” but it kept reappearing, like a spelling bee that refuses to advance to the next word. My father stood below, older than he is now, holding the flashlight upside down so the light pooled around his feet like a puddle he was afraid to step in. I wanted to tell him it was okay, that I had forgiven the fan for wobbling, the flashlight for cracking, the darkness for existing. But I could not speak; my mouth was full of clay shaped like a bowl that refused to be useful. I woke up with the word “forgo” stuck to my tongue like a stamp that never quite sticks to the envelope.

I decide to repaint the bathroom because I read that small acts of control can stave off existential dread. I choose a color called “Whisper of Wheat,” which sounds like a secret told by gluten. I paint around the mirror, leaving a frame of the old color—an accidental mirror border that makes me look like I am always standing in a portal. I practice threshold speeches while brushing my teeth: “Welcome to the Kingdom of Almost, population you.” I wink at myself, but the牙膏 foams and ruins the suave effect. I spit and rinse and decide that tomorrow I will count the brush strokes instead of sheep. Maybe then I will sleep without ceiling fans or flashlights or bruises that bloom like midnight violets.

My mother’s Sunday call comes early; the neighbor’s cat is missing. She is convinced it has been kidnapped by someone who wants a feline locksmith. I soothe her with theories of territorial roaming and mating calls. She is not comforted. “That cat knew how to open doors,” she says, “it could have gone anywhere.” I picture the cat sailing through thresholds like a furry astronaut, unbothered by physics or grief or gratitude. I want to tell my mother that I too have been kidnapped, by a version of myself who counts steps and avoids thirteen and sits on stairs counting bruises. But I do not want to worry her; she has enough to worry about with a cat who can pick locks.

I hang up and stand in the bathroom, staring at the portal frame around the mirror. I raise my hand and wave at myself like I am greeting a neighbor I have not seen since the ceiling fan days. “Hi,” I say, “I’m here to report a missing person.” The mirror-me raises an eyebrow, a small act of bureaucratic resilience. I lean in and whisper, “I think she was kidnapped by a version of herself who knows how to open doors.” The mirror-me nods, a small act of maternal defiance against the international date line of grief. I decide that tomorrow I will build a bowl for both grief and gratitude, and I will fill it with words like forgive and forget and forego, and I will place it on the kitchen table next to the monument to the art of almost getting it right. It will sit there, empty, a bowl that refuses to be useful, a threshold that refuses to be crossed. And I will count its emptiness like a rosary made of midnight, a small act of bureaucratic resilience against the darkness that exists, the flashlight that cracks, the ceiling fan that wobbles, the bruise that blooms like a midnight violet, the cat that opens doors, the mother who calls early, the mirror that frames a portal, the bowl that refuses to be useful, the word forgo that sticks to the tongue like a stamp that never quite sticks to the envelope, the envelope that never quite reaches the destination, the destination that never quite exists, the existence that never quite ends, the ending that never quite arrives, the arrival that never quite happens, the happening that never quite ends, the ending that never quite arrives, the arrival that never quite happens, the happening that never quite ends.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
